data.title: Luckin Coffee: Evidence Of Cannibalization As Comps Erode (Downgrade)
data.description:
We've landed in the middle of the Q1 earnings season, and with the stock market already sitting near all-time highs and investors nervously attuned to macro volatility, we're in for a potentially bumpy quarter. In my view, it's a good time for investors to reshuffle our portfolio holdings, especially as fundamental stories change.

Luckin Coffee Inc. (LKNCY), in particular, is a stock I've soured substantially on since the start of the year. The Chinese coffee chain, which is aiming to take on Starbucks (SBUX) as the world's default coffee brand with new locations outside of China, has jumped ~6% after posting Q1 results. I, however, only see ongoing risks in the business as comp sales fade and margins compress.

I last wrote a neutral article on Luckin in March, when the stock was trading slightly higher in the mid-$35s. After digesting the company's Q1 results and growth slowdown, however, I'm coming away more disappointed with the company's execution. Luckin is prioritizing "growth at all costs," but in my view it's sacrificing not just profitability, but actual per-store performance. I'm downgrading the stock to a Sell rating.

In my view, Luckin is now a grab basket of red flags. The core risks to me include:

  • Clear evidence of cannibalization as Luckin opens stores too quickly. Yes, it's true that Luckin has its sights set beyond China. And yet its pace of openings may be too dense in certain markets. The company is currently opening stores at a pace of 28 per day, or more than one per hour. At the same time, comparable sales are declining, which is a reflection of a still-choppy global macro and potential cannibalization of existing locations.
  • Increased reliance on delivery is taking a toll on Luckin's margin profile. Though Luckin is hyper-focused on expanding its store network of both company-owned and franchise locations, in reality a rising mix of Luckin's sales is actually from third-party delivery platforms, which is forcing Luckin to pay out platform and delivery fees. This is dragging down the company's operating margins.
  • Tremendous competition overseas. Luckin's presence in overseas markets, especially in the U.S., is still brand new. There's a large chance that foreign consumers may reject Luckin in favor of local incumbents (the company has a reputation for funky flavors that may be off-putting to many coffee drinkers). In addition, in markets like the U.S., fast food giants like McDonald's (MCD) are stepping up their drinks game, signaling that new markets may not be that easy to crack.
  • Sharp increase in marketing expenses. The company is under pressure to keep up its advertising and social media presence. Sales and marketing expenses are rising much more quickly than revenue, which, on top of higher delivery expenses, is also contributing to margin erosion.

In my view, it's now best to sell off your Luckin position and move to the sidelines. I expect a tough global macro will wear down comp sales trends at other coffee chains like Dutch Bros (BROS) that have not yet reported results; in my view, it's best to reduce our exposure to names that are subject to volatile consumer spending.

Q1 Recap

Let's now go through Luckin's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Luckin's revenue grew 35% y/y to RMB 12.0 billion, or roughly $1.74 billion. Notably, the company is benefiting from a larger contribution of franchise/partner sales, with franchise revenue up 45% y/y to RMB 3.02 billion, roughly a quarter of the company's total.

Luckin's principal growth engine remains store expansion. In Q1, the company opened 2,548 net-new stores, or 28 per day (and more than 1 per hour). It ended Q1 with 33.6k global locations, rising 39% y/y.

33.4k of these stores are in China, of which 35% (11.7k) are operated by partners. It's in overseas store openings, however, that the company has set its sights. As of the end of Q1, the company has 177 locations overseas, which breaks down as follows: 82 company-owned stores in Singapore, 12 company-owned stores in the U.S., and 83 franchise-operated stores in Malaysia.

This brings me to my largest concern surrounding Luckin: with the company's all-encompassing focus on raw store growth, it may be sacrificing efficiency. Luckin is running into a similar problem that Starbucks did in the U.S.: running too many stores and cannibalization. Under new CEO Brian Niccol, Starbucks has been reversing that trend since last fall, closing many underperforming locations in the U.S.

This is clearly evidenced in Luckin's consistent deceleration in same-store sales. In Q1, same-store sales (which measures the revenue growth from existing stores, excluding the contribution from newly opened locations) slid to a -0.1% decline, from 1.3% growth in Q4 and mid-teens growth in Q2/Q3.

But at the same time as the company is investing heavily into new store openings, we note that a greater revenue share is being driven by delivery orders. When pressed on same-store sales in the Q&A portion of the Q1 earnings call, CEO Jinyi Guo acknowledged a very competitive backdrop in delivery orders:

So recently, the delivery war has created very strong growth opportunities in China's coffee consumption. So accordingly, we've been in the phase of a relatively rapid expansion, and this momentum continued into the first quarter. So in this backdrop, some level of fluctuation in our same-store performance is normal. And also, this is well in line with our business expectations. Having said that, same-store sales for our self-operated stores remained broadly stable in the first quarter. We believe it also reflects the resilience of our overall operations."

As a result of the boosted delivery mix, delivery expenses as a percentage of revenue rose to 10.8%, reflecting 380 bps of y/y declines. This coincides with a 340 bps y/y decline in store-level operating margins (excluding corporate overhead costs) to a slim 13.6%, again demonstrating declining efficiency per store:

Also, the hugely promotional and competitive coffee environment has forced Luckin Coffee to step up its marketing and advertising, where nominal expenses rose 48% y/y to RMB 733 million, far outpacing 35% revenue growth and even the 39% y/y growth rate in store count. As a result of lower store operating margins and sharply higher advertising expenses, Luckin's net income declined -3% y/y to RMB 506 million in Q1.

So again, we have to ask ourselves: if Luckin is counting on delivery orders for a greater share of sales (and sacrificing margin to do so), is it optimal for the company to continue building out new stores so aggressively? Its top-line growth is merely an illusion if growth comes at the cost of its bottom line.

Upside Risks, Valuation, And Key Takeaways

In my view, there are more risks than opportunities for Luckin as it exits Q1. That said, we also have to be cognizant of the upside risks that the company enjoys.

We note that Luckin's balance sheet is a major advantage in the company's favor. Luckin has the equivalent of ~$1.30 billion of cash on its balance sheet while having no debt. In other words, the company has quite a long runway and war chest to keep driving unit growth in international markets. Its deep pockets may also allow the company to drive deeper promotions and strategically sacrifice near-term profits to dominate market share in core Chinese markets.

Another potential upside driver for the stock is valuation. Trading at ~8x forward adjusted EBITDA, Luckin is trading far more cheaply than all of its U.S.-based peers, at half the EBITDA multiple of Dutch Bros and Starbucks.

That said, we are very wary of Luckin's negative same-store sales trends and its decline in net income. Sell this stock and invest elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Asset Bubbles Cast Long Shadows
data.description:
The Canadian Real Estate Association (CREA) has downgraded its forecast for home sales in 2026, while the number of homes sold across the country in March fell 2.3% from a year earlier.

CREA now expects a total of 474,972 residential properties to be sold in 2026, 1% more than in 2025 and down 30% from the cycle peak of 667,000 home sales in 2021.

In March, the national average sale price fell 0.8% from a year earlier to $673,084, down 18% in nominal terms and about -30% in real terms from the peak of $816,720 in February 2022. Nationally, the nominal Canadian average home sale price is back to its 2021 level. On a real basis, Canadian home prices have retraced 9 years to 2017 levels (shown below, courtesy of CEIC data and the BIS).

The organization of realtors expects the national average sale price to rise 1.5% to $688,955 in 2026 — about $10,000 lower than their January forecast.

On current forecasts, 2022 peak prices are not expected to be recouped in nominal terms until 2029 (WOWA).

After the 1989 bubble peak, home prices in the Greater Toronto Area (GTA) fell 28.5% in nominal terms until 1995. In real (inflation-adjusted) terms, prices did not return to their 1989 highs until 2011 — 22 years later.

China has been leading a global real estate cycle down after its massive property bubble burst in 2021. By 2023, Chinese housing starts had dropped more than 60% from pre-pandemic levels, marking one of the largest housing busts globally in decades.

China’s real residential property price index has seen a record 17 consecutive quarters of decline. In real terms, home values are now where they were in 2006, wiping out 20 years of appreciation for the country’s urban middle class (below via CEIC and the BIS).

There are some striking similarities between fiscal and monetary policy choices made in China and Canada. The following summary should ring some bells for Canadians:

China’s current property crisis stems from the central role of real estate in its economy, which drove rapid growth and contributed about 20% of economic activity. For a decade before the pandemic, housing prices surged relative to household incomes, partly because consumers, facing limited attractive savings options, increasingly invested in property. This fueled heavy borrowing by developers, while local governments became dependent on land sales for revenue.

In 2020, to address concerns about the overheating market, the Chinese authorities introduced the “Three Red Lines” policy aimed at curbing speculative behavior and excessive corporate debt. The policy imposed strict limits on developers’ leverage and liquidity, while banks were restricted from lending to these firms. This credit squeeze then contributed to the collapse of overleveraged developers.

…As property sales plummeted, many developers who relied on pre-sales to finance construction struggled to finish projects. In response, numerous homebuyers launched a “mortgage boycott”, refusing to make payments on unfinished apartments.

This decline was exacerbated by homebuyers’ concerns about developers’ financial health and uncertain property prices, with demand reaching a 13-year low.

In many countries where prices ballooned before the pandemic, home affordability remains untenable for the masses, suggesting prices have further to retrace.

EPB Macro is tracking similar implications for American home prices, employment and the economy, in "The Housing Recession That Never Came — And The One That’s Quietly Underway". Here’s a taste:

“…with the affordability problem in the housing market still extreme, the pace of new home sales will remain sluggish, forcing companies to continue reducing construction activity or carry even more completed inventory.

In either case, the residential construction cycle still hasn’t turned higher. It’s been the same cycle since 2022, just massively drawn out by major construction backlogs and extreme profit margins in the aftermath of the pandemic.

The broader economy will be relatively unimpacted until we see the cycle play out further in residential construction, with material job losses, because it’s the early-stage job losses in sectors like construction that create the problems for the rest of the economy.”

Baby boomers are today aged 63 to 80 and will all be over 65 within 4 years. This cohort owns the largest share of real estate and stock market assets, and they are increasingly looking to downsize holdings to reduce risk, upkeep, and overhead while increasing free cash flow. They need able buyers. Prices lie somewhere between what current owners hope to sell at and what younger cohorts are able and willing to pay.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Waste Management Is A Money Printing Compounder
data.description:
In mid-February, I reiterated my buy rating on Waste Management, Inc. (WM) for the second straight time and called the stock a top defensive pick for 2026. I cited margin expansion, robust cash flows, and attractive capital returns as the reasons for my bullish call. You can see in the chart below that there have been modest declines in the stock since then. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has actually advanced by around 4.6%. With their latest earnings results having come out just yesterday (April 28th), today I'll be providing a fresh analysis to see whether the stock remains a top defensive pick given the current environment.

Below, it is shown that Q1 was a respectable quarter for Waste Management. Revenue growth slowed, but strong expense control meant that margin saw expansion. Furthermore, while the top line underperformed expectations, the bottom line was able to show outperformance. Mixed annual guidance was reaffirmed, but capacity expansion shows that long-term growth is intact. Meanwhile, cash flows continue to be robust, and the stock actually isn't expensive when looking at the P/CFO and P/FCF ratios. Therefore, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on Waste Management stock.

Growth Slows

Let's get started today with a look at the top-line numbers. For 2026 Q1, Waste Management reported revenues that totaled $6.227 billion, up 3.5% YoY. This is a notable slowdown of growth from 2025 Q4's 7.1% growth, and so overall there has been deterioration in the trajectory of their business momentum. Furthermore, they actually missed analyst expectations by $60 million to show underperformance in the quarter. While this is, of course, disappointing, there are some encouraging signs for investors.

For core price and yield, Waste Management reported 6.3% and 3.9%, respectively. Back in full year 2025, the core price was 6.3% as well, but the yield was at 3.8%. This modest closing of the gap between these two key figures shows that overall the pricing power of the company has improved somewhat.

One of the key reasons why there was growth deceleration was the fact that collection and disposal volumes saw a 1.5% decline. This volume weakness was

primarily due to the impacts of harsh winter weather, intentional shedding of lower-margin residential business, and wildfire cleanup activities that benefitted the prior year period.

In my view, these are reasonable explanations for the softness and indicate that the top-line growth deceleration is less concerning than it seems. The improved yield shows that customers value their services, and that's probably the largest takeaway here.

Expense Controls Are Strong

As a percentage of revenues, adjusted SG&A expenses increased by 10 basis points YoY to 11.1% YoY to show minor inefficiencies in their corporate activities. Despite this, overall expense controls were shown to be strong, as Waste Management was prudent with their operating expenses. As you can see in the table above, adjusted operating expenses as a percentage of revenues dropped by 70 basis points YoY to show that productivity has strengthened. In the earnings release, CEO Jim Fish mentioned "cost optimization" and these numbers clearly indicate that the business is generally well run. Signs of robust profitability also showed up in their margin and bottom line.

The strong discipline with regards to managing operating expenses was clearly reflected in Waste Management's adjusted operating EBITDA margin. As shown above, this key profitability metric expanded from 29.1% in the year-ago quarter to 29.8%. While this improvement is less dramatic than what we saw back in 2025 Q4, margin expansion is almost always good news, and so investors should still be encouraged. Lastly, Waste Management reported an adjusted EPS of $1.81, which represents an 8.4% jump YoY and beat expectations by $0.07. The fact that they missed top-line analyst estimates but beat on the bottom line clearly shows that their profitability is outperforming.

Cash Flows & Capital Returns

In my previous article, I found robust cash flows, and this time around, Waste Management didn't disappoint either. Operating cash flows saw a 24.3% increase YoY. This far outpaces both the top and bottom line growth rates to show that their cash generation ability has been strong. With the phrase 'cash is king' being heard consistently, I believe the numbers here are promising. Furthermore, with CapEx receding from levels seen in the year-ago quarter, FCFs rocketed by a whopping 93.7%. This was clearly a driver of strong capital returns.

While the dividend yield still isn't the highest, I believe investors seeking capital returns should be happy with what they're seeing. Just in early March, the company raised their quarterly dividend by 14.5% to $0.945 per share. As a whole, Waste Management returned almost $730 million to shareholders, which represents the majority of their FCFs seen in the quarter. To be specific, the company paid out $385 million in dividends in Q1, up from $336 million in the prior year period, and bought back $344 million worth of stock versus no repurchases in the year-ago quarter. This shows that the strong cash flows are really paying off handsomely for investors.

Outlook Reaffirmed

After missing on the top line and beating on the bottom line, Waste Management decided to reaffirm their full-year 2026 outlook instead of providing any revisions. While I already talked about these expectations in my previous article, I will go over some highlights again here.

For revenue growth, they project a midpoint of 5.2%, which is a major deceleration from 2025. This was in line with consensus. While reasonable explanations were provided for the softness in Q1, it seems that the trajectory for business activity won't be overly favorable in the near term. For adjusted EBITDA margin, the expectations for a 30 basis point expansion show that prudent expense management will continue. Lastly, for FCFs, they are projecting a midpoint growth of 29.4%, so sizable capital returns should continue.

Despite the slightly mixed near-term guidance, I would say there are signs that long-term growth remains intact for Waste Management. In their Q1, operations reportedly began at their new recycling facilities in Ontario and Detroit. Furthermore, they completed a recycling automation project in South Florida. As a whole, Waste Management states that these projects add almost 300,000 tons of processing capacity, and with recycling seemingly getting ever more important, I would say that the company is well positioned for the long haul.

Valuation Analysis

While Seeking Alpha's valuation page shows that Waste Management is quite expensive according to most multiples, the stock's price tag isn't that high relative to the cash flows being generated. With cash being king and cash flows being an important metric for the company, I believe there are still justified reasons why investors should find the risk/reward to be net attractive right now.

For the P/CFO ratio, the 15.1 reading is a pullback from highs seen earlier this year. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha shows that this multiple is at just a 4.41% premium to the Industrials sector as of this writing. For a premium compounder, that isn't an unreasonable price tag to pay. If you look at their forward P/CFO ratio, Waste Management is actually at a discount to the sector. As for the P/FCF ratio, you can see above that the multiple is very near multiyear lows.

For a slow and steady company like Waste Management, cash flows tell investors a lot about the business' health. While most valuation multiples currently show that the stock isn't cheap, the potentially most important ratios do show that current levels are attractive for entry. Therefore, I continue to view the risk/reward as favorable for the bulls right now.

Conclusion

Waste Management stock hasn't performed as strong as I envisioned back in my previous article, but I believe there are enough reasons for long-term investors to remain bullish. Revenue growth weakness is a risk to be aware of, but it is important to note that the company is intentionally shedding their lower-margin residential business. With expenses getting controlled in a nice manner and cash flows showing robust business health, I believe the company remains well positioned. As discussed above, when looking at the cash flow-based valuation multiples, this premium business actually isn't trading at an overly premium price tag. Therefore, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on Waste Management stock.

data.title: U.S. Battery Storage Needs Innovation For Long-Term Growth
data.description:
By Coco Zhang, ESG Research

Battery energy storage systems (BESS) are set to gain strong momentum in the US, as outlined in our Renewables Outlook for 2026. However, growth projections for 2026 vary widely, ranging from 48GWh (BloombergNEF) to 70GWh (Solar Energy Industries Association, or SEIA). Our view is closer to that of Wood Mackenzie, which expects installations to grow from 54GWh to 56GWh.

Where actual growth lands will depend largely on how well the industry manages supply chain risks – more specifically, shaking off its heavy reliance on China.

In the long term, however, structural cost reduction will be critical as tax credits phase out. We believe that is best achieved through technological innovation.

Supply chain gaps leave BESS projects vulnerable

Similar to solar, US BESS manufacturing is heavily concentrated in downstream assembly, with limited coverage of upstream components. SEIA estimates that while the US has 79GWh of nameplate battery pack manufacturing capacity as of early 2026, battery cell capacity stood at just 22GWh, highlighting a significant upstream gap.

US BESS manufacturing capacity across the supply chain

GWh

Moreover, these figures include producers that make both stationary BESS and EV batteries, so the nameplate capacity dedicated to BESS alone is smaller. Plus, production can run well below nameplate capacity due to upstream bottlenecks. These two factors suggest that US BESS manufacturing has low self-sufficiency. Wood Mackenzie estimates that US battery cell manufacturing capacity could only meet 6% of domestic demand in 2025, though that share could increase to 40% by 2030. The scale of the projected ramp‑up is evident: 116GWh of battery cell capacity is currently under construction in the country.

Tax credits are the lifeline for US BESS to trump cheap Chinese imports

Manufacturing and clean electricity tax credits are crucial to the growth of domestic BESS manufacturing. Without either, US producers would struggle to compete with low-cost Chinese imports.

Even under the current tariff regime, US four-hour turnkey battery systems using batteries that benefit from the 45X tax credits are still 31% more expensive than systems using imported Chinese batteries. The gap will further widen when the tax credits start to phase out.

Cost of US 4-hour turnkey battery storage systems

$/kWh, usable (real 2025)

It is only when the 45X tax credits are combined with 48E/48Y clean electricity tax credits in four-hour energy storage projects that US projects using US-made batteries can enjoy a cost advantage.

Cost of US 4-hour BESS projects

$/kWh, usable (real 2025)

This means US battery manufacturers will need to double down on technological advancement and innovation. Based on current technology projections, tax credit phase-outs in the next decade will more than offset the cost advantage among US producers.

But before manufacturing capacity increases meaningfully across all supply chain components, the US BESS industry will remain exposed to tariffs and foreign entity of concern (FEOC) restrictions that target China. Below, we analyse these risks and how they can be managed.

China is still king in US solar imports, but with a weakening role

It is difficult to quickly dismantle China’s dominance, which accounts for nearly 90% of global lithium-ion battery manufacturing capacity. It will take time for the US to ramp up domestic manufacturing, and in turn, it will still need to rely on imports in the near term.

China accounted for the lion’s share of US lithium-ion battery (both EVs and non-EVs) imports in 2025, at around 60%. Although import tariffs on Chinese batteries are higher than elsewhere, the difference has not been as material as seen in solar equipment. And because of safety control, batteries are harder to be transhipped via a third country. Rising tariffs have therefore led to a decreasing share of Chinese imports without much transshipment from proxy countries.

US import of lithium-ion batteries by origin

% (based on $)

Here's what we'll be watching in terms of tariffs and trade looking ahead:

  • Further tariff hike on batteries: It is worth watching whether tariffs on Chinese lithium-ion batteries, currently at 25% under Section 301 (to prevent unfair practices), will be raised further. This year, the US initiated an investigation under Section 301 after the Supreme Court struck down reciprocal tariffs imposed, citing the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA).
  • Further tariff hike on graphite: Graphite is a key metal in lithium-ion battery production. Natural graphite imports from China currently face a 25% tariff; any further hikes under Section 301 would mean higher supply chain costs for battery developers.

Because of limited battery transshipment from China and decent battery pack capacity in countries like South Korea and Japan, US BESS developers have some ability to shift away from Chinese imports. In fact, battery pack imports from South Korea and Japan rose in 2025 and could increase further this year.

The bigger constraint, however, is battery cells, where alternatives to China remain far more limited. We discuss this in more detail below.

FEOC rules to have far-reaching impact on BESS supply chain

BESS developers will be subject to FEOC rules for longer, as BESS tax credits extend into the next decade, while solar and wind face near‑term eligibility deadlines. This means BESS developers will need to more structurally transition away from reliance on China.

In February, the Treasury Department released draft guidance clarifying how FEOC requirements can be met from a supply chain perspective. The Treasury is expected to issue further guidance on definitions of foreign prohibited entities (PFEs). Below is a summary of the BESS material assistance cost structure, while our detailed FEOC analysis can be found here.

Treasury’s material assistance cost ratios to determine FEOC compliance

%

If a developer now uses imported Chinese battery packs, it will need to replace them with US-made or non-Chinese imports. However, using US-produced battery packs does not eliminate FEOC risk. Developers must still verify that suppliers are free of Chinese influence across ownership, IP licensing, supply chain material assistance, etc.

Among these, battery cells are the most critical component, as they represent about half of the total cost used in the FEOC material assistance test for grid‑scale BESS.

This poses a significant challenge. China controls more than 80% of global battery cell production, while US cell capacity remains limited. On top of that, BloombergNEF estimates that roughly half of the current US operating BESS cell capacity may have high FEOC exposure risk due to corporate structure and IP partnership. That could further constrain near-term compliant supply.

Nevertheless, conditions can improve. Only 17% of planned US cell capacity is expected to face high FEOC exposure (excluding supply chain considerations), suggesting a meaningful expansion of compliant supply over time.

In the near term, rising demand for FEOC‑compliant components is likely to push prices higher. Many developers may prioritise FEOC compliance over domestic content eligibility. Longer term, progress will hinge on the pace of vertically integrated battery manufacturing growth in the US and among non‑Chinese partners.

Car manufacturers’ repurposing EV batteries can boost BESS capacity

As EV tax credits are phased out under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) and demand for battery storage from data centres accelerates, manufacturers are shifting their focus from EV batteries to BESS.

Ford (F), for example, has ended its EV battery joint venture with SK On and is repurposing its Kentucky plant to produce stationary LFP batteries, positioning the site as a BESS hub and exploring licensed CATL technology. GM (GM) is redirecting excess and used EV batteries to partners such as Redwood Materials and SunPower (SPWR), supplying BESS for customers, including AI data centres. Stellantis’s (STLA) joint venture with Samsung SDI (SSDIY) has made a similar shift in production priorities.

These strategic moves have reshaped industry expectations, prompting SEIA to double its forecast for US BESS capacity by 2030.

Conclusion

US BESS growth in 2026 looks strong, but efforts to avoid Chinese components could limit expansion. This underscores the need to scale domestic manufacturing across the value chain and, more importantly, to accelerate technology‑driven cost reductions to improve US competitiveness.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Adobe: Its Opportunities With AI Outweigh Its Risks
data.description:
Introduction

Like other SaaS companies, Adobe’s (ADBE) stock has been punished by speculative theses, and we are now seeing its stock trade at levels not seen in years. Adobe is a company with a moat praised by investors because it has a high-margin business with predictable cash flows and high switching costs. While the AI risk is real, I think there are many misconceptions about its threats and opportunities that may result in mispricing. In this article, I will discuss the threats and opportunities of AI and what the market may be pricing in.

Adobe's Moat

Just like AutoCAD is for architecture and engineering students, Adobe's ecosystem is for art and design students. The whole cycle of companies using Adobe and hiring people who use Adobe causes it to be part of the curriculum for schools and universities. It is a system that requires a deep understanding and many years of experience to be able to apply it in a meaningful way and is part of the reason why it is so hard for a competitor to gain market share. Adobe is an integrated suite that shares assets, integrates workflows, and is often used together in pipelines, making it incredibly hard to switch from. Apart from being the industry standard, it goes down to file formats being proprietary, such as files done on Photoshop being .PSD and on Premiere being .PRPROJ. With the ability to build on top of this software through the use of presets, plugins, templates, fonts, etc., users build their own personal ecosystem inside Adobe.

What I just talked about is Creative Cloud, which belongs to Adobe’s Digital Media segment. This segment represents 74% of Adobe’s revenue and has a gross margin of 95%. 25% of its revenue comes from its Experience Cloud, which “enables businesses to create, manage, execute, measure, monetize, and optimize customer experiences.” It has the advantage of connecting customer data, content creation, and marketing execution while also having its creation tools. With its Experience Cloud having a gross margin of 72%, it is also evident that it has a great moat that, combined with Creative Cloud, has enabled Adobe to have the profitability and growth metrics that it has.

This moat has given Adobe flexibility with its business model and pricing. First of all, it enabled Adobe to remove perpetual licenses and charge a subscription for its Creative Cloud suite. This is a massive benefit, as it provides very predictable and macro-safe cash flows that investors favor. Adobe has also increased its subscription prices across Creative Cloud multiple times along with introducing price tiers. By bundling its tools into Creative Cloud, Adobe has also managed to increase average revenue per user (ARPU).

Threats of AI

I think the main threat that Adobe has is that its moat is threatened and could eventually be commoditized. Adobe’s pricing is seat-based SaaS, meaning a business using Creative Cloud pays per user for the duration of the contract. AI will be able to absorb all the technical training that people go through to meaningfully use its tools. Just like Claude can basically do all of what a junior analyst can do in Excel, the same thing can happen with Photoshop, Premiere, and all other tools. AI could learn how to use and navigate these applications faster and more efficiently than humans, which could lead users to let AI manage the tools instead. It will either allow fewer people to do more with less, meaning that the number of seats decreases, or allow multiple people to use one AI agent through a single seat. Either way, in my opinion, its seat-based pricing will stop working the way it was intended to and will have to change to a usage-based pricing model. This means that the application layer will become commoditized, and annual recurring revenue may no longer be as relevant.

Another thing that AI threatens is that because AI would absorb the whole learning process, it will not only be able to use other competing tools with ease but also migrate existing ecosystems. A company will be able to migrate all of its .PSD files to another, cheaper competitor with minimal costs. Not only that, but it will also be able to migrate its entire ecosystem, including its plugins and data, across competing products. With lower switching costs and a usage-based pricing model, Adobe’s moat weakens, meaning its profitability could decrease and its option to raise prices becomes harder.

Another thing that I want to talk about is that because of its moat, Adobe has not had the same incentive to innovate or build a culture around rapid innovation. In my opinion, Adobe’s whole DNA is built around the idea that they don’t need to move fast because the moat handles it for them. Adobe is facing the problem that its own organizational structure will prevent them from responding fast enough. While R&D expense has almost doubled since 2021, from $2.5B to $4.2B in 2025, as a percentage of revenue, it has not meaningfully increased. I do not see clear evidence of aggressive competitive spending, as Adobe’s R&D-to-revenue ratio of 18% is still below the SaaS average of 27%. CapEx spend has a similar story, with its CapEx/Revenue ratio historically being below the sector median of 2.33%.

Opportunities

Lowering switching and training costs also provides Adobe with the best opportunity to reach more customers. In my opinion, this is a classic example of Jevons paradox, which says that when something becomes more efficient to use, people end up using it more. Everyday people with no experience in Photoshop will not spend money on it if they cannot use it. People will have the opportunity to do more creative work, design companies will be able to accept more projects, and more people will be able to improve the quality of their social posts, presentations, and videos. It is the same reason why now that LLMs have basically solved programming, more people are creating more things, and vibe coding is becoming very popular. AI-generated code worldwide has grown sixfold over the past two years, from 5% in 2022 to nearly 30% by the end of 2024, and it is probably way higher now. We are already starting to see this, as in Q1 FY 2026, Adobe’s president of creativity & productivity business, David Wadhwani, said the following:

“Notably, creative freemium MAU crossed 80 million, growing 50% year over year and includes web and mobile versions of Firefly, Express, Premiere, Photoshop and Lightroom. Second, AI usage continues to grow quickly, as measured through record levels of generative credit consumption.”

I do not think professionals will switch to editing only through LLM prompts when they can use AI add-ons inside Photoshop, where the AI will have more capabilities. Adobe also has the data advantage for its Creative Cloud and its Experience Cloud that no LLM has and can actually use to train its AI.

Adobe has announced partnerships with a number of AI companies. Notably, Anthropic, NVIDIA (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG), and OpenAI. From what I have seen from these partnerships, Claude and other LLMs will have to communicate directly with Adobe’s Firefly, which will use the whole set of Adobe’s tools. The problem that I see with this is that there are still many unanswered questions, such as how Adobe will monetize it. Will add-ons work the same way? Will Adobe be able to stay in the orchestration layer or be reduced to a tool API?

I think the real question investors have to ask themselves is whether the increase in demand will outweigh the reduction in pricing power and profitability. And, which outcome is currently being priced by the market?

Valuation

For the valuation, I created a DCF that models my thesis. For revenue growth, I considered a reduction in Adobe’s ability to raise prices, offset by an increase in demand for its products now that the learning barrier is removed. Historically, its revenue 5Y CAGR was 11%, and with the added uncertainty of how its partnerships will look and how usage-based pricing will affect it, I assume 12% growth. With Adobe’s moat under pressure, I expect margins to decrease with its gross margins eventually reaching 85% and operating margin 25%. To reach an operating margin of 25%, I also assume that CapEx and R&D will significantly increase to levels a bit lower than the industry average.

With all of these assumptions, I get an implied share price of $300. If I had to guess, the market is probably also pricing in a reduction in revenue growth, which I don't see. The main issue with this model is that it does not consider Adobe switching to a usage pricing model, which is an important part of my thesis. I would have guessed that a usage pricing model would affect its top line more than its margins, but because of the lack of information regarding seat economics, I would only be guessing.

Conclusion

AI represents a significant threat to Adobe’s moat through lower switching costs and, most importantly, its seat-based pricing model. Investors will no longer be able to value the company with ARR, as the predictability of its cash flows will decrease. A threat to its moat will mean a threat to its stellar profitability metrics and an increase in CapEx and R&D. On the other hand, Adobe has the most capable suite of tools and ecosystem out of any competitor. It makes no sense for a company to switch tools if it already knows the large capabilities of Photoshop, Illustrator, and InDesign. The value of these tools is not dependent on their price but on what users can do with them, and AI will unlock much more value from it. I also believe that productivity increases will increase the demand for these products, as it always happens. At an implied value of $300 per share, I think the market is pricing in a revenue decline likely from the switch to a usage-based pricing model. The issue that I have is that it is too soon to know how Adobe will react to AI and what pricing model it will apply to each service. We also do not know how its partnerships with AI models will work, and I am worried that Firefly could become much worse than competing models, causing users to avoid it and making it more of an obstacle than an advantage. I think the market is misunderstanding the threats and opportunities of AI, which I think results in a clear mispricing. I rate it a Buy.

data.title: AMD Is Better Positioned Than Intel To Profit From Agentic AI (Rating Upgrades)
data.description:
AMD And INTC: My Notes From INTC's Q1 Earnings Report

My last analysis on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) stock was published on Mar 18. The article was triggered by AMD’s then-new deals with Meta (META) and OpenAI (OPENAI) and rated the stock as a Hold. As for Intel Corporation (INTC) stock, my last coverage dates further back to Dec 11, 2025. The article focused on the use of options to hedge the near-term uncertainties facing the company and rated the stock as a sell. With the rapid evolution of AI technologies, much has happened to both companies. In this article, I will concentrate on the most fundamental change in my view—the new role of CPUs for AI deployments as revealed in INTC’s recent Q1 2026 earnings report (ER) released on April 23. AMD is scheduled to release its Q1 2026 ER on May 5, and I believe the insights provided in INTC’s ER are also relevant to AMD.

With this background, the goal of this analysis is twofold. First, I want to explain how INTC’s recent FQ1 ER foretells the rising demand of CPUs for AI inference infrastructure, which could lead to sustainable demand for CPU manufacturers (i.e., in addition to GPU makers). And secondly, I will argue that AMD is better positioned than INTC to benefit from this potential demand. These considerations have led me to upgrade my ratings on both stocks, with INTC rated as a Hold and AMD as a Buy.

Let me start with a brief recap of INTC’s ER for readers unfamiliar with it yet. The company reported normalized EPS of $0.29 on total revenue of $13.6 billion, both beating analyst expectations (of $0.28 and $12.4 billion, respectively). As noted in the ER slide below, this is the 6th consecutive quarter INTC exceeded consensus expectations. Management also commented that CPUs are “foundational to inference and agentic” AI. A comment that aligns well with my own observation that CPUs are becoming increasingly more important in AI inference. The AI industry has mostly been focusing on GPU-centric training thus far, where GPUs are highly efficient. But I believe the expansion of AI agents will require a balanced approach where the CPU handles tasks that GPUs are not so efficient at, such as sequential and/or load-type tasks.

AMD And INTC: R&D Investments

My above outlook for CPUs role, of course, is a positive for both INTC and AMD (and other CPU stocks too). Between these two leading CPU stocks, I see AMD as better positioned strategically to benefit from this tailwind for many reasons. To keep the discussion on point, I will limit my discussion to the top two on my list: architectural scaling and R&D efforts. AMD’s chip architecture and memory performance are superior to INTC for AI agentic applications in my view. Agentic workflows are heavily dependent on cache size and memory bandwidth to reduce the latency of multi-step reasoning, and AMD’s EPYC series outpaces Intel’s comparable chips (say, the Xeon series) on both fronts in my assessment.

Beyond their existing products (like EPYC and Xeon), I am also more optimistic about AMD’s future products than INTC. In a nutshell, I consider AMD’s R&D efforts to be more coherent—thus more efficient as a result - than INTC’s. On the surface, Intel maintains a larger R&D budget both in absolute dollars and relative to its sales. To wit, the R&D budget at INTC was approximately $13.5 billion in 2025 (and even higher in the few years before that), compared to “only” about $8 billion at AMD. As another illustration, the next chart shows the R&D expenses of AMD and INTC in recent quarters as a fraction of their total revenues. As seen, AMD on average has been spending about 24.6% of its total revenue on R&D efforts, compared to about 28% at INTC.

However, more inputs don’t always lead to more outputs, as detailed next.

AMD And INTC: R&D Yield

Despite INTC’s much larger R&D expenses, the R&D yields are quite low compared to AMD in my assessment, as shown in the next chart. In this chart, I used a variation of Buffett’s $1 test on the R&D expenses of AMD and INTC. The method is detailed in our earlier articles, and I will directly quote the end results here:

The purpose of any corporate R&D is obviously to generate profit. Therefore, it is intuitive to quantify the yield by taking the ratio between profit and R&D expenditures. This way we can quantify how many dollars of profit has been generated per dollar of R&D expenses. In this chart, I used the operating cash flow as the measure for profit. Also, most R&D investments do not produce any result in the same year. They typically have a lifetime of a few years. Therefore, this analysis assumes a 3-year average investment cycle for R&D. And as a result, we use the 3-year moving average of operating cash flow to represent this 3-year cycle.

As seen, the R&D yield for INTC has been in the negatives in recent years. Before 2021, INTC featured an average R&D yield of more than $1. This means every $1 of R&D input had led to more than $1 of profit again in those years. However, the R&D yield has worsened drastically since then and stayed negative till 2025. Per the latest Q1 2026 data, I saw the first positive yield since 2022, which could suggest a trend reversal thanks to the demand for CPUs.

Whether Q1 2026 marks a turning point for INTC or not, the yield from AMD has been much stronger in recent years. As seen, AMD has reported a positive yield every year since 2018. Looking ahead, I expect AMD’s yield to remain superior as its R&D is consolidated around a single and scalable architecture. In contrast, Intel is managing its R&D efforts both on chiplet architecture and also manufacturing capabilities. And its chiplet efforts are further divided into three separate AI architectures (Gaudi for accelerators, Xeon for servers, and Core Ultra for PCs).

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of valuation, the potential demand tailwind certainly comes at a price. As seen, both INTC's and AMD's valuation ratios now exhibit a significant premium either in absolute or relative terms. AMD’s FY1 P/E on a Non-GAAP basis is about 48x, and INTC’s reading is even higher at 78x. In contrast, NVDA, the leading GPU stock, is trading at 25.5x. However, when adjusted for growth, AMD’s PEG (P/E growth) ratio (again on a Non-GAAP basis) is about 1.13, and Intel’s PEG is about 1.07. Both ratios are quite reasonable by the standard of GARP investors (growth at a reasonable price).

Before closing, let me point out a counterargument to my above thesis. My above analysis on the R&D yield considered INTC’s manufacturing efforts as a drag. It has been a drag thus far in my view, and I keep seeing little odds for it to compete with TSMC on the most lucrative orders. However, there is a potential for INTC’s manufacturing capabilities to be highly profitable. Besides the possibility of a major technological breakthrough, this potential could also materialize if geopolitics change in a way that “Sovereign Supply” of AI chips takes priority.

To close, this article is motivated by the changing role of CPUs for AI deployments revealed in INTC’s recent Q1 2026 ER. AMD is expected to report its Q1 ER in about a week, and I believe the observations made from INTC’s can be a leading signal for AMD’s performance. These observations have uplifted my view and ratings on both stocks, and I see a more skewed return/risk ratio for AMD after considering their product lineups, R&D efforts, and also valuations.

data.title: AMLP: The AI Investment Hiding In Plain Sight Yielding 7.63%
data.description:
Many individuals spend a tremendous amount of time looking for investment ideas where the narrative hasn't fully caught up to the fundamentals. The Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) is one of those ideas at the moment. AMLP is an ETF that holds a basket of master limited partnerships, including Energy Transfer (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). I believe that the AI data center build-out is a structural tailwind for the midstream sector as they have become fee-based tollbooths for moving traditional sources of energy throughout the country. AMLP has a dividend yield of roughly 7.63%, and the underlying business model has been quietly de-risked as the demand curve for U.S. natural gas just got rewritten by AI data centers and a Persian Gulf war that is now in its ninth week. I think that midstream operators are still undervalued, as they are the arteries of America, moving natural gas, crude, and refined products from point A to B. AMLP allows you to own the basket of midstream without having to worry about the K1 tax implications because of the ETF wrapper and generate a large single-digit yield at the same time. AMLP has been a great investment YTD, and I believe shares will continue higher as 2026 progresses.

Following up on my previous article about AMLP

At the beginning of December, I wrote an article on AMLP (can be read here) where I discussed why I was bullish on this ETF heading into the new year. AMLP’s holdings generate stable, fee-based cash flows that are insulated from commodity price swings and were supporting yields that exceeded 8%. AMLP is an ETF, so the K-1 tax complexity was eliminated, and investors got an income-producing asset with a standard 1099. I felt that MLPs would continue to run as the demand for energy continued to grow. We’re in a significantly different world at the end of April than we were in December, as there is a war in Iran and WTI is around $100 per barrel. I believe that American LNG will become the standard overseas for building up stockpiles of energy since not every nation is a net producer of energy. The combination of domestic demand from data centers and other countries purchasing LNG from American companies should drive the volume of energy moving through the systems of AMLP’s companies higher. I believe that MLPs will collectively be reset with higher valuations, which should drive AMLP higher while collecting a large dividend.

K-1 Tax Package

The purpose of a K-1 form is for each partner to report their share of a business's gains, losses, and distributions to the government. MLPs issue a Schedule K-1 to their investors. To read more about Schedule K-1, please click here. AMLP is an ETF that provides exposure to MLPs that issue a K-1, but the actual ETF does not issue a K-1 to its shareholders. Please click here to read about the tax advantages of owning AMLP.

The AI Build-Out Is A Midstream Story

Income from any asset is only as durable as the cash flow it's producing. When that cash flow is fragile, future distributions are at risk of declining. This is where I see AMLP standing apart from a lot of high-yield products. Its holdings aren't speculative early-stage names chasing a thesis; rather, they're established midstream operators sitting on hard assets that make the American energy system run. Companies such as Energy Transfer, and Enterprise Products Partners own pipelines, storage tanks, fractionators, and processing plants. This is infrastructure with a useful life measured in decades rather than quarters. A meaningful share of that infrastructure operates under long-term take-or-pay or fee-based agreements where these MLPs get paid based on volumes flowing through the system rather than commodity spot prices. This strips a lot of the direct commodity sensitivity tied to OPEC headlines and the spot economics of crude out of the equation. The revenue these operators collect correlates much more closely with overall energy demand.

I am continuously researching the energy markets and read through many of the Energy Information Agency’s (EIA) reports to see which direction production is expected to go. In the latest Short-Term Energy Outlook from the EIA, it looks like U.S. natural gas production over the next year and a half will increase from 121 Bcf/d to 127 Bcf/d, while LNG gross exports increase by 20-25%. By the end of 2027, U.S. crude upstream production is expected to exceed 14 million BPD. Most midstream cash flow is now contractually fee-based rather than commodity-exposed, and the business model is closer to a regulated utility than it has been in the past. When I look at the charts below and read through the EIA report, it becomes simple reasoning. As more energy is produced, more oil and gas will have to be transported, treated, and stored throughout the systems within AMLP’s holdings. The revenue should increase, the distributable cash flow should increase, and the distributions should increase, which should make AMLP a solid investment.

The amount of capital being deployed by hyperscalers right now is unlike anything we have seen in modern infrastructure history. Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META) are collectively guiding to roughly $700 billion in 2026 CapEx. In 2023, just as the ChatGPT era started, the combined CapEx from these hyperscalers was $140.13 billion. In 2024 this increased by $77.14 billion, or 55.04% YoY. In 2025 the CapEx allocation increased by $140.24 billion or 64.55% YoY. In this fiscal year, the total CapEx is expected to increase by an additional $251.49 billion, or 70.35% off the 2025 base. This is very bullish for MLPs because data centers need energy to run. Without energy, they are just very expensive buildings with minimal to no use. As more data centers come online, the amount of energy needed to power them will expand, and more fuel will need to move through AMLP’s underlying company systems. This is a trend that I believe will last for several years, and AMLP should benefit as the MLP sector is a direct beneficiary of the AI buildout.

The Income Case For AMLP Goes Beyond The Headline Yield

The 7.63% yield certainly catches the eye, but the yield by itself doesn't tell investors anything about whether that income is durable. Plenty of high-yield products have looked attractive but then disappointed investors when their dividends were reduced or the NAV eroded at a pace where the income couldn’t offset the losses. What separates AMLP from the broader high-yield universe is that the yield is being supported by a group of energy infrastructure companies that have contractually locked-in revenue streams rather than businesses chasing growth that may not materialize. When I look at the alternatives in today's income market, the comparison gets interesting quickly. The United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) is currently yielding around 4.4%, and the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) yields roughly 3.37%. MLP roughly doubles those payouts and does so with a portfolio of essential infrastructure rather than concentrated bets on financials, healthcare, or consumer staples that face their own demand and regulatory pressures.

The distribution coverage on AMLP's underlying holdings is what gives me confidence that the yield is real income rather than an unsustainable payout waiting to be cut. Midstream operators today are running distribution coverage ratios well above 1x, which is a different reality than the 2014-2016 period when the sector was overlevered. ET, EPD, and the rest of the AMLP basket have spent the last several years deleveraging balance sheets, simplifying corporate structures, and rebuilding coverage cushions. The result is a sector that is producing more free cash flow than it's distributing and entering this AI demand cycle with the financial flexibility to grow payouts rather than defend them.

That distribution growth angle is something I think the market is underappreciating. AMLP isn't just a static yield as the underlying MLPs have been raising their distributions at mid-single-digit annualized rates, and the volume growth I outlined earlier should give them the runway to keep going. AMLP is paying $3.97 per share and has increased the dividend for four consecutive years. The five-year consecutive growth rate on the dividend is 6.56%, and this could continue to come in at a single-digit growth rate for years to come. AMLP delivers a yield that nearly doubles what investors would collect from broad dividend ETFs, and it makes owning the largest MLPs much easier without getting issued a K-1. This combination is why I view AMLP as one of the more attractive risk-adjusted income vehicles available heading into the back half of 2026.

Risks to investing in AMLP

While I am bullish on AMLP, there are several risk factors to consider. The first risk is energy demand itself, as my investment thesis revolves around increased levels of traditional fuels flowing through midstream systems. If hyperscaler CapEx growth slows due to AI workloads becoming more energy efficient, then the volume thesis won’t hold up. Midstream cash flows are fee-based, which insulates the sector from spot commodity price swings, but they aren't insulated from volume declines. If the Fed decides to raise rates due to inflation increasing, it would make the cost of capital more expensive for MLPs and create an alternative in the risk-free markets with bonds. There are also regulatory and political risks to consider, and while the current environment may be favorable for energy infrastructure, that could change in the future. Investors should do their own due diligence before allocating capital to AMLP.

Conclusion

The hyperscalers are spending hundreds of billions of dollars on data centers that need to be powered, and the EIA is forecasting that upstream production of traditional energy sources will increase over the next several years. I think the setup for AMLP looks interesting because MLPs are likely to be indirect beneficiaries of the AI revolution that can deliver both upside appreciation and pay a large single-digit yield to investors. AMLP is the AI investment hiding in plain sight, as the infrastructure represented in its portfolio will become more valuable as more assets are dependent on their systems. I believe that AMLP will continue to be a hybrid investment that will continue to generate income and capture some capital appreciation in the future.

data.title: Pzena International Value ADR Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Non-U.S. equity markets saw mixed results in the first quarter, as a spike in oil prices combined with AI disruption fears led to heightened uncertainty following a very strong 2025. Value stocks continued to outpace growth stocks, in part due to higher energy prices. Our portfolio outperformed the broader market index during the quarter.

Energy, materials, and utilities were the portfolio's top-performing sectors.

Equinor, a major natural gas supplier in Europe that serves as an alternative to imported crude oil, and Shell, the world's largest trader in LNG (liquefied natural gas) with sourcing outside of the Persian Gulf, rose, driven primarily by stronger oil and gas prices amid escalating Middle East tensions. Memory chip manufacturer Samsung continued to benefit from rising demand for conventional DRAM (dynamic random-access memory) along with an increased likelihood of its high-bandwidth memory chips being used by leading AI companies. German chemical producer BASF gained as the company released earnings in late February and provided a 2026 outlook, alongside signs of improving pricing and the completion of a major investment project in China. Finnish telecom equipment manufacturer Nokia was higher on investor optimism surrounding its AI-RAN prospects, while its network infrastructure unit continued to exhibit solid growth.

The consumer discretionary, industrials, and communication services sectors all detracted from absolute performance in the quarter.

IT services company Accenture's recent results have been weak amid cyclical IT spending headwinds, fueling market concerns about deflationary pressure from AI. We believe these fears are overstated; moreover, we believe Accenture remains an essential facilitator of enterprise digital transformation. Household products and personal care company Reckitt Benckiser declined after reporting disappointing results characterized by muted volumes and weakness in its European and U.S. consumer product businesses. Nonetheless, Reckitt's restructuring remains on track, with both cost reductions and product innovation expected. Although Japanese medical device company Olympus has been affected by product quality issues and recently provided an earnings forecast below consensus expectations, the new management team is overseeing a meaningful restructuring of the business.

During the quarter, we initiated multiple new positions. LVMH (LVMHF), the world's leading luxury conglomerate with a collection of 75 brands across fashion, jewelry, cosmetics, spirits, and retail, has been impacted by a decline in Chinese demand across categories, as well as a broader slowdown in overall volumes, exacerbated by recession fears. We believe Chinese demand weakness will stabilize as real estate prices trough, leading to improving consumer sentiment. We also initiated a position in United Utilities (UUGRY), a regulated water and wastewater utility in the northwest region of England. The UK water sector is under pressure due to an adverse regulatory environment and negative public sentiment regarding sewage pollution from storm overflows. Given the significant need for capital to remedy years of underinvestment, we believe increased spending will ultimately drive multi-period rate base growth. We also initiated a position in Magnum Ice Cream (MICC), a spinoff of Unilever (UL) with the largest global market share (18%) of any competitor, and a range of brands including Magnum, Breyers, and Ben & Jerry's. Magnum's earnings have been weak due to rising raw material costs and declining growth related to higher GLP-1 uptake. We expect both margins and volumes to recover, as out-of-home consumption grows globally, fueled by increased travel and growth in developing markets. We also expect Magnum to further penetrate at-home consumption channels (grocery stores) where it has been underrepresented. We also initiated a position in Porsche (POAHY), a leading luxury automaker. The company is currently suffering from a drop in sales in China. Additionally, Porsche overestimated demand for its electric-only models. Sales should improve as old models are refreshed with combustion engines and new models are released into new market segments. Finally, the company's strong brand should help it sustain strong margins through the cycle. Finally, we initiated a position in Bangkok Bank (BGKKF), a deeply discounted, overcapitalized Thai bank where cautious lending, margin pressure, elevated reserves, and a weakening macroeconomic backdrop have weighed on earnings and masked the underlying earnings power of the franchise.

We added to our existing positions in Accenture (ACN), Publicis (PUBGY), and tractor manufacturer Kubota (KUBTY), all on weakness. Publicis, like Accenture, has been caught up in the AI-disruption selloff that characterized this quarter. Kubota is facing near-term demand weakness in the U.S., as well as uncertainty around tariffs and exchange rate fluctuations. While the company navigates these short-term headwinds, Kubota is also working on self-help measures to further improve its profitability, offering us the opportunity to buy a strong franchise at an attractive valuation.

The constantly shifting macroeconomic environment and uncertainty over AI's evolution have resulted in elevated dispersion between individual stocks. With respect to AI, investors' fears are spreading across multiple sectors, and we expect to continue finding opportunities in areas where these concerns are particularly acute and potentially overstated. Our portfolio has sizable exposures to businesses in profit recovery mode, especially in Europe. Overweight sectors include health care and consumer discretionary, while information technology remains underweight due to high valuations, at least for the time being, as many companies have recently sold off due to AI disruption fears.

data.title: April Fed Meeting: Powell's Final Call, Rates Steady
data.description:
The last several Open Market Committee (“FOMC”) meetings of the Federal Reserve (“Fed”) have been fairly mundane. There were no adjustments to existing rate policies and current Chairman Jerome Powell’s commentary largely stuck to a continuing script, modified ever so slightly meeting to meeting.

April’s FOMC meeting was largely expected to follow the same pattern, with one exception. Being Powell’s final meeting as Chair, observers were clamoring over one significant question of interest. Would Powell stay or leave the Fed after his term ends?

To the disappointment of some from a political level, Powell did confirm that he would continue to serve as a governor after his term ends. The meeting, otherwise, was much of the same, for the most part. Interest rates held steady, and the commentary was more-or-less in-line with messages previously communicated.

Leading into the release, market direction wasn’t necessarily dictated by the fate of the meeting. Equities were more focused on continuing developments involving the war with Iran and a large slate of Q1 earnings reports, including from a slew of tech behemoths.

All three indexes, including the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), Nasdaq Composite (NDX), and S&P 500 (SPY), edged lower following a positive start as these developments rolled in throughout the day.

In my view, today’s FOMC meeting met my expectations with regards to the rate policy and corresponding commentary.

Here’s a further dive into the relevant pieces factoring into April’s Fed meeting.

How Healthy Was The Labor Market Prior To The April FOMC Meeting?

In my view, March’s jobs report did exactly what the Fed needed it to do, and that is balance out the poor reading from the month prior.

In March, the economy added 178K jobs, a sharp rebound from February’s revised loss of 133K jobs and far ahead of expectations for just 59K additions.

Those taking a bullish political posture on labor would likely say that this is a labor market that is re-accelerating. In reality, however, the reading reflected more of normalization after weather distortions and a healthcare strike reversal than a true surge in hiring.

In terms of unemployment, the rate has been holding steady for the most part. In March, the overall rate dipped to 4.3%, but that came alongside a drop in labor-force participation to 61.9%.

It’s worth noting that prime-age participation remains near multidecade highs. To me, this suggests that demographics and immigration trends and not worker discouragement are driving the decline.

Zooming out, job creation has clearly downshifted, averaging roughly 20k/month. Yes, that’s slow, but still enough to keep unemployment steady.

For the Fed, this was ideal. The labor market wasn’t overheating, but it also wasn’t deteriorating. This likely enabled policymakers to leave rates exactly where they are.

How High Was Inflation Prior To The April FOMC Meeting?

If the labor data removed pressure from the Fed through its balancing act, the March CPI report likely made it crystal clear why rate cuts were off the table. And it probably contributed to the number of dissents on the forward outlook, in my view.

Headline inflation jumped to 3.3% YOY, up sharply from 2.4% in February and the highest reading in two years. The culprit was somewhat obvious to most market observers. Energy.

Gasoline prices skyrocketed nearly 19% and broader energy costs climbed 12.5% as the war with Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz sent oil prices soaring.

But beneath the surface, the picture was calmer. Core CPI, which strips out food and energy, rose just 2.6%, This was actually a touch below expectations. Outside of energy and transportation-linked services, inflation looked relatively contained.

That nuance likely was important for the Fed in its decision making. Inflation was clearly too high to justify cuts, yet not broad-based enough to demand hikes.

With energy driving the spike and second-order effects still limited, I believe that policymakers had every reason to stay steady and leave benchmark rates unchanged.

What Was Expected For The April FOMC Meeting?

Heading into the April FOMC, there was virtually no debate about what the Fed would do. Rate futures reflected a near 100% probability that policymakers would leave benchmark rates unchanged. This probability could be seen by the CME FedWatch Tool.

In my view, sticky 3% inflation, elevated oil prices, and a labor market that was soft but stable left little room for either a cut or a hike. The consensus among economists was simple, and that was simply ‘sit tight.’

That lack of suspense shifted attention squarely to Jerome Powell. With this marking his final meeting as Chair, observers were far more interested in what he might signal about his future than about monetary policy.

Would he remain on the Board after May? Would the DOJ probe and confirmation of Kevin Warsh influence that decision? In my view, Powell’s press conference carried more “what’s next?” intrigue than “what’s the rate path?” anticipation.

What Changed Following The April FOMC Meeting?

In actuality and with all things considered, not much changed, and that was the primary story.

The Fed left rates untouched. The policy statement was word smithed but largely similar to prior meetings. There were no meaningful tweaks to forward guidance, no removal/addition of language suggestive of the next move, and no hawkish pivot despite hotter inflation data.

From a pure policy standpoint, April looked and felt like a copy-and-paste of the last several meetings.

One detail of note, and a big one, is the number of dissents on the actual policy path ahead. With the decision split 8-4, and with many favoring a more neutral outlook on rates as opposed to dovish, that could signal some turbulence among the governing board in the new era with Warsh.

All that said, what stood out more to me was Powell's decision to remain on the Fed as a governor. The decision to stay is an unusual one, considering most before him have decided to retire on to private life.

The decision is also significant because it essentially deprives President Trump another vacancy on the governing board during the remainder of his term.

Main Takeaways From The April FOMC Meeting

In my view, the Fed’s April meeting ultimately followed a theme we’ve seen for months. That is, steady policy, steady messaging, and a Fed content to wait.

To me, the Fed isn’t being forced into action by either side of its dual mandate. The labor market is cooling but intact. Inflation is elevated but largely being driven by energy rather than a broad reacceleration. That combination appears sufficient to keep policymakers comfortably on hold.

For this specific meeting, what mattered most wasn’t any change in rates or language, but the confirmation that the current posture remains appropriate in the face of ongoing macro uncertainty.

To that end, Powell’s final press conference as Chair didn’t deviate from his established script. He did, however, inject some new drama into the equation with his decision to stay on as a governor after the end of his term.

Dissents on the policy front also show further division in the broader policymaker front and that could signal more turbulence ahead.

For markets, my takeaway is that monetary policy is no longer the dominant near-term driver. Instead, I believe that macro crosscurrents, such as geopolitics and earnings resilience are and will continue to set the tone.

That said, my broader equity view remains unchanged. I continue to find defensive positioning as appropriate, and I believe that selectivity will remain a key market theme as we move through 2026, just as I laid out in my market outlook article published at the end of 2025.

data.title: BNY Mellon Global Stock Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The conflict in the Middle East is driving up energy prices and injecting turbulence into asset markets, disrupting what had been a broadly constructive macro backdrop. It is important to stress that we have no direct exposure to Middle Eastern companies, and that revenue exposure to the region is not significant at the portfolio level.

Potential outcomes in terms of economic growth and corporate earnings will largely hinge on the duration and extent of the conflict. At present, the scene is set for a structurally higher oil price, given the infrastructure damage to energy facilities and the Iranian closure of the Strait of Hormuz to all but a few ‘friendly’ powers. Investors will have to weigh the impact of a sustained rise in energy prices on the consumer, inflation, supply chains, and businesses.

The pivot to ‘risk-off’ since the start of the conflict was alleviated only periodically by signs that the U.S. was looking to de-escalate the conflagration. U.S. and most European markets retreated sharply from record highs, although Japan managed to eke out a small gain.

“Volatility is likely to remain elevated, and we continue to monitor developments closely, alert to any signs of deterioration in the long-term fundamentals of the companies we hold.”

Performance Summary

The BNY Mellon Global Stock Fund underperformed the MSCI World Index for the first quarter of 2026.

The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Performance Review

The Fund lagged its benchmark. The rise in oil and gas prices drove strong performance in the energy sector, and the Fund's lower exposure to energy was a key detractor from relative performance.

The healthcare sector detracted notably in absolute and relative terms. Much of the underperformance was due to relative industry exposures. Specifically, greater exposure to weak healthcare equipment & supplies, and life sciences tools & services and a less-than-benchmark weight in the comparatively strong pharmaceuticals industry. The consumer discretionary sector was the weakest within the index and the Fund's greater exposure to the sector weighed on relative return.

Industrials holdings trailed their index peers. The professional services industry has suffered due to ongoing weakness in software-related stocks, as advances in AI were seen to pose potential disruptive threats to companies in the space. The Fund's greater exposure to the industry was a relative detractor.

On the upside, despite the share price weakness in software names, technology holdings outperformed and were key contributors to relative return. Held materials companies demonstrated strong performance over the quarter and contributed in absolute and relative terms.

Top Contributors

The top contributors to relative performance include ASML Holding (ASML), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Shin-Etsu Chemical (SHECY), Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), and Linde (LIN).

Top Detractors

The top detractors from relative performance include Microsoft (MSFT), Adobe (ADBE), LVMH (LVMHF), Booking Holdings (BKNG), and Experian (EXPGF).

Market Outlook

Against this backdrop, we take confidence from the fundamental resilience of the companies held in the Fund, which we believe is underpinned by strong balance sheets and reliably cash generative business models. These qualities tend to come to the fore during periods of macroeconomic and geopolitical stress. That said, volatility is likely to remain elevated, and we continue to monitor developments closely, alert to any signs of deterioration in the long-term fundamentals of the companies we hold. It is also the case that at times like these, opportunities can arise for the long-term, bottom-up investor.
